title: Identify the GHL workspace
example: Use a demo dashboard screenshot and label the left navigation, sub-account area, contacts, conversations, calendars, funnels, and automations.
steps: Open a GHL demo, screenshot, or training image.
Point out where contacts, conversations, calendars, funnels, and automations live.
Write one sentence explaining what kind of business problem each area solves.
Save or upload a screenshot proof for your notes.
expectedOutput: A short explanation of the main GHL areas
One example client request matched to the correct area
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of the main dashboard with the left navigation visible.
testingNotes: Can you explain where you would start if a client asks for faster lead follow-up?

title: Design clean tags and custom fields
example: For a dental clinic, create tags like source-facebook-ad, interest-implant, status-new-lead, and custom fields like Preferred Appointment Time.
steps: List the lead source, interest, and follow-up status tags needed for the client.
Decide which information belongs in a custom field instead of a tag.
Open a contact record or screenshot and identify where tags and custom fields appear.
Create a quick test contact plan using realistic but fake data.
expectedOutput: A screenshot or visual reference of the contact record
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of a contact record showing tags, fields, notes, or tasks.
testingNotes: Check that tags describe groups or status, while custom fields store specific values.

title: Map a lead into pipeline stages
example: For a real estate team, use stages like New Lead, Contacted, Appointment Set, Offer Sent, Won, and Lost.
steps: Write the business sales process from first inquiry to closed deal.
Turn each major step into a pipeline stage.
Choose when an opportunity should move manually and when automation can move it.
Create one fake opportunity card and decide which stage it belongs in.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of pipeline columns and one example opportunity card.
testingNotes: Check that every stage describes a real business step, not just a vague status.

title: Plan a booking flow
example: For a pet grooming shop, create a booking flow with grooming service length, buffer time, reminder messages, and a no-show follow-up.
steps: Choose the appointment type and length.
Set the available days, business hours, and buffer time.
Write the confirmation and reminder message content.
Describe what should happen when someone no-shows.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of calendar availability, booking settings, or a booking link preview.
testingNotes: Test with a fake appointment and confirm the reminder timing makes sense.

title: Build a lead capture path
example: For a clinic ad, create a form with name, phone, email, service interest, and preferred appointment time, then send the lead to a thank-you page.
steps: Choose the minimum fields needed to qualify the lead.
Decide which fields should be required.
Plan the thank-you page message and next step.
Write where the form should send the lead inside the CRM.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of the form builder, funnel page, or thank-you page.
testingNotes: Submit fake data and check that the lead arrives in the expected place.

title: Draft reply-aware follow-up
example: For a new dental lead, write a first SMS, an email fallback, and a reply handling rule so the workflow stops when the lead responds.
steps: Write a short SMS that sounds human and asks for one clear next action.
Write an email version for leads who do not respond by SMS.
Identify where replies appear in the Conversations inbox.
Decide when staff should take over manually.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of a mock or redacted conversation thread.
testingNotes: Check that messages do not continue after a lead replies or books.

title: Design workflow steps
example: Build a missed-call workflow with a trigger, SMS response, internal notification, wait step, if/else branch, and stop condition.
steps: Choose the trigger that starts the workflow.
Add the first action the lead should receive.
Add internal staff notification or task creation.
Add a wait step and condition for reply or appointment booked.
Write the stop rule that prevents over-messaging.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of the workflow canvas showing trigger and actions.
testingNotes: Run a fake test contact through the workflow and inspect every step outcome.

title: Prepare a launch checklist
example: Create a checklist for client requirements, CRM fields, form testing, workflow testing, staff handoff, and final launch notes.
steps: Collect the client requirement in plain English.
List the GHL assets to create or update.
Write the fake-data test cases.
Write the handoff notes a staff member would need.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of your checklist, settings screen, or QA notes.
testingNotes: A teammate should be able to follow your notes without asking what to click next.

title: Plan an external automation
example: Send a seller valuation lead from GHL to Make, n8n, or Zapier, then create a Google Sheets row and Slack notification.
steps: Decide what stays inside native GHL workflows.
List the exact fields to send to the external tool.
Identify the webhook or integration trigger.
Write the external steps and error handling plan.
Write how you will protect API keys, tokens, and webhook URLs.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep a screenshot of the webhook action or external automation scenario.
testingNotes: Send mock data first and verify the destination before using real client leads.

title: Package proof into a case study
example: Turn one practice build into a short case study with problem, solution, screenshots, testing proof, and a resume bullet.
steps: Choose one client-style problem you solved.
Add screenshots or diagrams from your build.
Write the workflow or CRM logic in simple language.
Add testing proof and what you would improve next.
Write one resume bullet from the project.
screenshotPrompt: Upload or keep screenshots of the final workflow, form, pipeline, or case study page.
testingNotes: The portfolio proof should make sense to a non-technical business owner.
